text: Good morning today we will learn how to use the da Vinci robot for continuous suturing first you have to make a dandy jamming knot outside and that is actually a loop within loop and then you take the bite and after taking the needle out the needle should be taken from within this dandy jamming knot so that without making a C and reverse C you will have a starter knot and you can start with the locking the knot here we can see just by pulling the suture the loop will start becoming smaller and then you can introduce inside and then you can take the suture out care should be taken that the needle end of the suture has to be taken out if you will take the non-needle end out it will get locked so the needle end of the suture has to be pulled in da Vinci robotic surgery suturing you can use the endoskeletal as well as the half circle needle also now you will pull it and it will automatically get locked and then you have to start your continuous suturing for continuous switching every time making a C is very useful so that you should take the byte within the C this is a normal speed video we have not increased the speed of the video just to demonstrate you carefully and within the C you will take the byte so that every time once you start the continuous switching it will keep on locking

you don't have to lock it and due to the wrist articulation you can pull it very nicely tight it and again take the another suture another bite and that way continuous suturing can be used for the serous surface of the myoma as well as we are using it for the gastric placation and sometime in hernia surgery for the peritoneal closure in da Vinci continuous suturing has particularly role in the cases of the radical prostatectomy and sometimes the partial nephrectomy for the closure of the capsule in those situation it will be faster and you will not have to use the multiple suture with one twenty four centimeter suture

The surgeon uses the da Vinci robotic system to perform continuous suturing along a fifteen-centimeter intratummy line, holding the needle at the junction of two-thirds and one-third. The needle is aligned by passing it from one robotic hand to another, following principles similar to open surgery.

The surgeon demonstrates the termination of a knot using the Aberdeen technique in da Vinci robotic suturing. The loop within loop method is applied, pulling the non-needle end to lock the knot. The suture is tightened, and the needle end is carefully handled to avoid trapping, completing the termination process.
